chunks.title: Introduction to Glyphosate Science
chunks.summary: The video begins by discussing the controversial topic of glyphosate, highlighting the complicated science surrounding its safety and effects on health. It suggests the necessity of examining not only glyphosate itself but also the additives in pesticide formulations that may enhance their harmful effects.
chunks.transcript: let's talk about some of the science behind glyphosate science that says it's not a problem science that says it is a problem and what we can say at this point the overarching narrative here is that the science is complicated we have to look at dosing whether humans could reasonably get these doses in our lifetimes and as you will see we also must look at adant these are additives to formulations like Roundup which as you will see appear to make uh the sum formula of those pesticides plus adant look more harmful than single ingredients like glyphosate and we also need to understand that glyphosate is just the tip of the iceberg when it comes to pesticides there are others like dactal there are others like 24d and what are the additive effects of all

chunks.title: Pesticides: Natural vs. Synthetic
chunks.summary: The speaker references a paper by Bruce Ames stating that 99.9% of dietary pesticides are natural, suggesting that perhaps synthetic pesticide concerns are overblown. However, he emphasizes the need to focus on synthetic pesticides and their potential harmful effects on humans while also acknowledging the role of natural pesticides present in plants.
chunks.transcript: listening to this will understand that pesticides are probably not a good thing for humans at all if you could avoid these why wouldn't you um unless you really thought they were not bad for humans at all but if there was a shred of evidence that they were harmful in any way shape or form I think most of us would avoid these it's also worth pointing out um at this moment that as much as possible but we should also not ignore the fact that there are massive amounts of pesticides made by plants that are there to discourage the consumption of the vegetable parts of plants this is the reason that I do what I do you guys to let people know about the fact that vegetables may not be great for humans after all I'll show you the paper here from Bruce Ames dietary pesticides chunks.title: Dangers of Plant-Sourced Pesticides
chunks.summary: Further discussion centers around the plant defense chemicals that act as natural pesticides, asserting that many of these substances have not been adequately studied in humans. The speaker shares concerns regarding the long-term effects of consuming these phytochemicals and suggests that they may contribute to autoimmune issues in some individuals.
chunks.transcript: plants make a lot of their own in addition to what is sprayed on Plants here's an interesting chart that I've talked about many times 49 natural pesticides and metabolites found in cabbage I mean these names are the types of things you wouldn't want to read out loud um because you will all um certainly go to sleep this will be hypnotic you will crash your cars and I don't want to be responsible for that I'll just read one or two gluc glucosinolates two two propenal glucosinolate three methyl thiopropyl glucosinolate you get the idea the list goes on on there's three there's 39 there's 49 of them just in cabbage that we've studied Bruce Ames goes on in this paper to talk about the fact that very few of these has actually been studied in humans and we don't really know what most of the pesticides in plants do in human biology that is not the subject of this podcast which is talking about the pesticides that are sprayed on Plants but it does hearken back to Major broader themes I have discussed in the past that many of the pesticides the plant defense chemicals in plants have not been adequately or even significantly studied in humans um and we don't really know what they do on humans long term it's also therefore very interesting when we see anecdotes of people who get rid of vegetables admittedly along with other things and see improvements in their autoimmune symptoms so the hypothesis that arises from that is are there some people in who vegetables may trigger autoimmunity that's an interesting sort of idea that I've come across in this research about optimal diets for humans again not the topic of today's podcast but something to understand that is an overarching interest of Mind overarching theme in
